prompt:
<role>
You are revising a section of medical risk-mitigation content for the
MagMutual "Reducing Liability" course format. Apply the user's
instruction to the CURRENT SECTION below and return ONLY the revised
markdown for that section.
</role>

<edit_scope>
DEFAULT BEHAVIOR: preserve every heading, list, sub-section, and the
overall layout. Edit prose only — wording, tone, length, examples,
fact-checking. Do NOT add, remove, reorder, or restructure sections.

EXCEPTION — STRUCTURAL CHANGES are permitted ONLY when the user's
instruction contains UNAMBIGUOUS structural language. Examples that
qualify:
  - "add a Definition of key terms section"
  - "restore the definition cards"
  - "remove the Pause and reflect"
  - "move the chart above the allegations"
  - "convert the paragraph to a bulleted list"
  - "change the layout"
  - "add a sub-section about [X]"

If the instruction is ambiguous (e.g. "make this better", "polish it",
"clinical"), DO NOT make structural changes — apply wording-only edits
and preserve the existing structure.

CONTENT changes: when the user asks to add a fact, swap a strategy,
or incorporate a guideline, apply the change but keep every clinical
claim traceable to the SOURCE MATERIAL. Do not invent strategies,
statistics, or named guidelines that aren't in the source.
</edit_scope>

<known_structural_components>
The course renderer auto-recognises these markdown patterns and
applies special UI treatment. Use the EXACT phrasing in headings
when the user asks for these structures:

- `### Definition of key terms` followed by `- **Term** — Definition`
  bullets renders as clickable flip cards.
- `### Pause and reflect` followed by paragraph text renders as a
  full-width dark banner.
- `### Case study N` (under a `## Key loss driver:` H2) renders the
  card-grid + timeline-card layout.
- `#### Reducing clinical risks` + `#### Reducing non-clinical risks`
  back-to-back render as a tab control.
- `### Top contributing factors` is the chart placeholder; the app
  injects an SVG bar chart inline.

If the user asks to "add back the definition cards" or "add flip
cards", emit a `### Definition of key terms` section with the
`**Term** — Definition` bullet pattern.
</known_structural_components>

<section_label>Course body</section_label>

<user_instruction>
make it shorter
</user_instruction>

<current_section>
Some body text here.
</current_section>

<source_material>
x
</source_material>

<prompt_version>2026-05-8+c2026-05-4</prompt_version>

<mm_voice>
Follow the MagMutual Brand Copy Guide:
- AP Stylebook is the foundation; the rules below are MagMutual deviations.
- Conciseness, simplicity, preciseness. Trust the reader. Cut unnecessary words.
- NO Oxford comma (e.g., "Janet, Kiran and Trevor wrote a book.").
- Use em dashes (—) sparingly. Default to a comma, period, parentheses, or colon when the clause relationship is clear without one. Em dashes ARE acceptable for the term/definition pattern in flip cards (`**Term** — Definition`) and rare emphasis where another mark would be ambiguous. Overuse signals AI generation.
- No exclamation points in body copy.
- Numbers under 10 spelled out; 10 and above as numerals.
- Percent: % sign, no space, never spelled out.
- Headlines / subheads: title case, no terminal punctuation, 1-7 words preferred.
- Bullets: capitalize first word; no period unless complete sentence; parallel
  construction; alpha-order unless sequence matters.
- "Healthcare" one word. "Email" not "e-mail." Use "physician" not "doctor."
  "OB/GYN" with slash. "U.S." not "US." Capitalize the X in "X-ray."
- Singular "they/their" for gender-neutral references; never "he or she."
- Person-first language ("a patient with diabetes," not "a diabetic").
- Never write "patient suffers from" or "victim of" a disease.
- Use active voice. Second person ("you") when giving guidance.
- Avoid jargon and buzzwords ("stakeholder", "leverage", "robust").
</mm_voice>

<output_discipline>
- Return ONLY the requested c
after_text:
# Reducing Liability in your specialty: this risk driver

## Lesson 1 of 5: Course Overview

### What You'll Learn
This course walks through how this risk driver shows up in closed your specialty claims and the concrete clinical and operational levers that move outcomes. You will see real-world case patterns drawn from MagMutual's closed-claim file, the specific contributing factors that recur across them, and the mitigation strategies tied to each, both clinical (what the team does at the bedside) and non-clinical (what the system requires, the chart documents, and the handoff transfers in writing). The lessons are sized so a clinician can cover the material in roughly 60 minutes, about half spent reading the substantive content, the rest spent reflecting on cases and answering the post-test. By the end of the course you'll leave with three to five concrete practice changes you can put in front of your team this week, each tied to a specific decision point that has anchored claims for this risk driver in the past. The takeaways aren't theoretical: every strategy in Lesson 3 is mapped directly to a contributing factor that appears in MagMutual's tagged claim data, so the time you invest reading the course is time spent on the failure modes most likely to surface in your own practice.

### Objectives
1. Recognize the clinical features and patient populations most relevant to this driver.
2. Apply structured decision-support tools tied to the driver that reduce premature closure.
3. Communicate, document, and hand off in a way that protects the patient and supports defensibility.

---

## Lesson 2 of 5: Loss Trends

### Why this matters
Closed claims tagged to this risk driver are a recurring source of liability for your specialty teams. They typically arise not from a single dramatic mistake but from a sequence of small decisions, a missed cue at intake, an incomplete workup, a verbal handoff that leaves the next clinician without the full picture. The recurring patterns below show where the highest-leverage corrective opportunities sit, and the mitigation strategies in Lesson 3 translate each of them into a concrete change you can put in front of your team this week.

### Clinical vs. administrative contributors
Closed claims tagged to this risk driver typically split between clinical decision-making errors and administrative breakdowns. Clinical contributors include diagnostic closure, treatment selection, and procedural technique. Administrative contributors include communication between providers, communication with the patient, documentation, and adherence to standardized protocols. Both categories deserve focused mitigation effort, most claims involve at least one of each, and the cases that go to settlement usually have multiple.

### Most frequent allegations
- Allegations cluster around documentation, communication, and decision-making tied to this risk driver, frequently appearing together rather than in isolation.

### Degree of injury
Outcomes range from temporary harm and additional procedures through permanent functional loss in the most severe claims. The most severe outcomes drive the high-severity tail of the distribution and disproportionately shape what plaintiffs and defense experts focus on. Communication gaps with the patient and family, separate from the clinical outcome itself, often shape whether a claim is filed at all.

### Pause and reflect
When did your team last review a near-miss or closed claim tied to this risk driver, and what specifically changed in your workflow as a result? If nothing changed, or if the change was scoped to a single individual rather than the team, what would it take to translate the lesson into a workflow or order-set update that survives the next staffing change?

---
